item.title: Surrey's top cop out, police board chair resigns amid SPS shakeup
item.description: The Surrey Police Board said Tuesday that Chief Const. Norm Lipinski had left the service. Soon after its announcement, police board chair Harley Chappell announced his resignation.
item.article_content:
It's been a chaotic day for the Surrey Police Service (SPS).

The Surrey Police Board announced Chief Const. Norm Lipinski has left the service, only for the board chair to resign in protest just hours later.

Now-former chair Harley Chappell said the board made the decision to fire Lipinski at a special meeting that Chappell did not attend.

In a news release, the board said it wanted to assure the community it remains focused on protecting public safety.

The board did not say why Lipinski, in its words, left.

Deputy Chief Const. Todd Matsumoto has been appointed as interim chief constable, according to the board.

The Vancouver Sun reported Monday that Lipinski had been told he had "until Thursday at 4 p.m. to resign or be terminated without cause from his position."

CBC News has not verified the Sun's reporting. It has reached out to Lipinski, the Surrey Police Board and Surrey city council for more information.

Norm Lipinski named first chief constable of Surrey Police Service

More stories from Vancouver-South Coast

The SPS and the provincial public safety ministry directed requests for comment to the Surrey Police Board, saying the board is Lipinski's employer.

Board chair resigns

In his resignation letter shared with CBC News, Chappell — who is the chief of the Semiahmoo First Nation — said he feels the decision violates the board's governance policies and the provincial Police Act.

"I feel the political tentacles and pressures have reached far too deeply into our newly formed SPS board, and I morally and ethically cannot be part of this moving forward," he said in the letter.

In an interview with CBC News, Chappell said he was assured that the police board meeting last Friday did not contain any motions and was for information only, and he did not attend it due to other commitments.

But Chappell said the board made the decision regarding Lipinski's removal at that meeting.

He said he does not think Lipinski was given a fair shake, and that his vision for the SPS was never able to come to fruition — due to crises like a rash of extortion cases , gang-related shootings and the pressure to complete the transition away from the RCMP as the police of jurisdiction.

"I don't like quitting. I'm not a quitter ... it is really, truly disheartening to make that decision," Chappell added.

SPS's first chief

Lipinski was named as the first chief of the SPS in 2020 as the city made its controversial transition away from the RCMP.

His biography on the SPS website says he previously spent five years as deputy chief of the Delta Police Department and 32 years as deputy chief of the Edmonton Police Service.

He also spent time as an assistant commissioner with the RCMP.

"Chief Lipinski is uniquely equipped to help steer Surrey through its transition from the RCMP to a municipal policing model," his online biography says.

Lipinski's departure comes just months after the province shook up the city's police board.

In February, Premier David Eby announced the province would not renew board members' terms as they ended. Five new members were appointed in March for 12-month terms. Eby suggested the whole board could be replaced.

The nine-director board, an independent civilian oversight body for the municipal police department, employs all sworn and civilian staff of the SPS, according to its website.

Surrey police board shakeup amid extortion crisis and police force transition 'bad timing,' chair says

Surrey has been dealing with a rampant extortion crisis, with 112 reported extortions and 18 related "shots fired" in 2026 as of May 25.

The police board said it will soon begin the recruitment process for a new permanent chief.

Union upset

The Surrey Police Union said it was concerned about the transparency around the end of Lipinski's tenure.

Union president Ryan Buhrig said in a letter to members, before the board confirmed Lipinski had left, that police board members were not returning his calls.

In the letter, which was obtained by CBC Newsm Buhrig questioned whether Lipinski is taking the blame "for challenges that were created by Mayor Brenda Locke, the City of Surrey, and the Province of British Columbia."

He added that "Chief Lipinski did not create the political instability surrounding Surrey policing."

"Many of the concerns our members have today are concerns he repeatedly raised himself."

Buhrig said staff shortages in the SPS "have reached critical levels," meaning officers and staff are expected to do more with less.

History of tension between chief, mayor

Locke issued a statement Tuesday morning thanking Lipinski for his service.

"I am confident that the Surrey Police Board has a plan in place that will continue to prioritize keeping our neighbourhoods safe," she said.

The relationship between SPS and Locke has been tense.

Locke was elected in 2022 on a campaign to keep the RCMP rather than transition to a municipal force. She was part of a lengthy battle against the province, including a judicial review , to reverse the transition that ended with her eventual acceptance of it.

At one point in 2023, while Locke was still fighting for the RCMP, Lipinski issued a public release criticizing some of her statements around increased taxes due to the policing transition. He called her statements "inaccurate" and "intended to confuse residents and business owners."

Earlier this month, in the wake of the shooting deaths of two teens connected to the B.C. gang conflict, Locke criticized an SPS decision to redeploy its gang unit to staff Surrey's Cloverdale district and called for a review .

Lipinski said the reassignment was temporary due to the extortion crisis and policing transition and would be back to its regular staffing in mid-June.

Premier David Eby addressed the news at an unrelated news conference Tuesday.

He said he hadn't yet received a full briefing from the police board but added that he told Locke months ago that "the province of B.C. has been too...

evaluation.reason_en: This is a significant local leadership change in the Surrey Police Service, but it is primarily an internal governance story with limited direct practical impact on daily life for most residents.
evaluation.persian_angle: تغییر رئیس پلیس سری برای ایرانی‌هایی که در سری زندگی می‌کنند مهم است، اما این خبر بیشتر یک تغییر اداری است و تأثیر مستقیم و عملی روی زندگی روزمره ندارد.

item.title: How steep are World Cup ticket prices for locals?
item.description: The price of tickets has turned the World Cup into an elite spectacle and data shows just how costly the tickets are for the residents of the host cities.
item.article_content:
The price of tickets has turned the World Cup into an elite spectacle and data shows just how costly tickets are for residents of the host cities.

While World Cup tickets are a hot commodity in many of the 16 host cities across North America, local economic disparities mean at least some fans would have to fork over weeks — or months — of disposable income just to attend a match.

Data from the Organization for Economic Co-operation and Development (OECD) shows that in the state home to Guadalajara, Mexico, average per-capita incomes are the lowest of any host region, yet the cost of tickets on the secondary market ranks among the most expensive of the tournament, according to the price-tracking site TicketData — likely pricing out many locals.

At the other end of the spectrum, those in San Francisco, located in one of the wealthiest states in the U.S., are paying some of the lowest ticket prices because of underwhelming demand.

Using these metrics, Toronto comes in as the fourth least affordable, with average income of Ontario on the lower end compared to American host cities, but ticket prices lower as well. Vancouver offers a slightly better deal. Despite B.C. residents having a similar income average to Ontarians, the lower ticket prices make securing a seat easier.

Eye-popping prices

Perhaps the most eye-popping prices emerge when comparing final match ticket prices to those of previous years. In New Jersey, Category 4 seats, (the most affordable option available for the final) were officially priced at $2,030 US following December’s draw. Even when adjusted for inflation, that's nearly nine times the cost of a comparable seat in 2022.

FIFA responded to fans who were furious about the cheap-seat prices by allocating a small number of $60 US tickets for each game. But recently, the governing body's president, Gianni Infantino, defended the sky-high prices, suggesting FIFA is recapturing revenue it would have otherwise lost to ticket scalping.

Should locals get affordable access in return for helping foot the bill for the World Cup? We'd like to hear from you. Click ‘Join the Conversation’ at the top or bottom of this story. On the app? Tap here .

The U.S. has the most developed entertainment market in the world, "s o we have to apply market rates," he said recently . “In the U.S. it is permitted to resell tickets as well. So if you were to sell tickets at the price which is too low, these tickets will be resold at a much higher price.”

Toronto resale tickets for World Cup no longer on FIFA site following new price cap rule

Some FIFA World Cup fans 'flabbergasted' by ticket prices in Vancouver

FIFA has employed dynamic pricing for this tournament, meaning prices are opaque and fluctuate based on market conditions. Back in December, the priciest Category 1 tickets were officially $8,680 US, and were quietly hiked to $10,990 in April, when FIFA also added even more expensive categories.

But a reminder, these are official prices for the final, not resale prices. The cheapest tickets to the final are likely hovering around $8,000 US, according to TicketData. It’s also been reported some are listed for more than $2 million on FIFA’s own resale site, which allows sellers to name their price while FIFA takes a 15 per cent cut from both the seller and buyer.

However, in April all Toronto tickets were removed from FIFA's marketplace after a new price cap came into effect. Ontario legislation bans people and platforms from reselling tickets for more than face value.

Resale market in Toronto, Vancouver

That doesn't mean tickets on the resale market will be cheap. For fans in Toronto who are simply looking to experience the atmosphere regardless of the matchup, resale data shows a fixture like Senegal vs. Iraq will cost hundreds. Those looking to support Team Canada in their clash against Bosnia-Herzegovina will have to pay up big time, with tickets pushing $1,000 as of this writing.

In Vancouver, the most accessible entry point on the secondary market is the New Zealand vs. Egypt match. Conversely, securing a seat for the high-stakes, yet-to-be-determined fixture on July 7 is currently north of $1,300.

Seeing Team Canada play seems cheap when you see the prices of other, more established teams.

At least one projection suggests these ticket prices will help FIFA take in $15 billion US .

The tournament begins June 11 with a match between Mexico and South Africa in Mexico City.

evaluation.reason_en: While locally relevant and from a trustworthy source, this is primarily a sports/entertainment cost analysis with limited practical actionability for the Persian-speaking immigrant community.
evaluation.persian_angle: این مقاله نشان می‌دهد که بلیت‌های جام جهانی برای ساکنان ونکوور چقدر گران است، اما اطلاعات عملی یا راهکار صرفه‌جویی برای جامعه ایرانی ارائه نمی‌دهد.

item.title: NHL faces scheduling challenge as B.C., Alberta move to permanent daylight time
item.description: The NHL will have a fresh challenge when it comes to setting its 2026-27 schedule with British Columbia moving to permanent year-round daylight saving time this fall and Alberta expected to follow suit.
item.article_content:
The NHL will have a fresh challenge when it comes to setting its 2026-27 schedule with British Columbia and Alberta moving to permanent year-round daylight saving time this fall.

The change will eliminate twice-yearly clock changes in those provinces. The time gap between B.C. and Alberta and parts of Canada that still turn clocks back on Nov. 1 would then shrink by an hour.

Game times for sporting events could be impacted, along with long-standing traditions like hockey doubleheaders.

"We'll deal with it," NHL commissioner Gary Bettman said Tuesday before Game 1 of the Stanley Cup final in Raleigh, N.C. "It may impact some start times in Calgary, Edmonton and Vancouver.

"But we'll deal with it just as we deal with whatever things come our way that we don't get to control."

THE BUZZER What to know for the Stanley Cup final

The NHL normally releases its schedule in mid-July. Regular-season play usually begins in early October.

"Hockey is still a big draw," said Chris Zelkovich, an instructor at the Toronto-based College of Sports Media. "But if you throw roadblocks at people like starting games at (different or) inconvenient times, you are going to lose audience."

Last March, British Columbia finalized its plan to make the change. Alberta followed suit last month.

Complication for Canadian doubleheaders

Although there are exceptions, Canadian teams based in the Eastern time zone — the Toronto Maple Leafs, Montreal Canadiens and Ottawa Senators — usually start their home games at 7 p.m. local time.

That has previously set up doubleheader timing with the teams based in Alberta and B.C. — the Calgary Flames, Edmonton Oilers and Vancouver Canucks — who would normally be two or three hours behind the ET clock.

Trimming that time difference by an hour may require some strategic scheduling. There could be an overlap of games, or we could see later start times in the west to protect back-to-back TV windows.

"This is going to be a major adjustment," Zelkovich said of potential changes. "And if your team is a Stanley Cup contender, that may not matter.

"But if they aren't, you might just say to yourself, 'Well, it's just not worth the trouble [to watch]."'

The Winnipeg Jets are in the Central Time Zone, one hour behind ET.

'Not rainbows every day': Canucks turn to Manny Malhotra for coaching answers

Blue Jackets' Werenski beats out Avalanche's Makar for NHL top defenceman honours

A lost hour would also be felt when the Flames, Oilers and Canucks play road games along the U.S. West Coast. For example, if the puck drops around 9:30 p.m. Alberta time, games may not finish until after midnight in that province.

"Ensuring hockey games remain accessible for fans in Oil Country is our priority," Oilers president and chief revenue officer Stew MacDonald said in an email. "And any shift that results in later game start times is a concern — particularly as it creates challenges for families and young fans attending games or watching live broadcasts."

The Flames declined to comment, while the Canucks did not return messages.

When asked for comment, a Sportsnet spokesperson said the league sets the schedule and start times. A TSN spokesperson said the network had nothing to share.

"I don't think it fundamentally changes their business," said Mike Naraine, an associate sport management professor at Brock University. "It might, from an advertising perspective, cause [broadcasters] a little bit more [work].

"It's not a problem, but just another thing that they just work through."

The CFL, which has nine teams across the country, also uses regular doubleheaders. The regular season ends Oct. 24, so the league won't really be impacted this year.

evaluation.reason_en: This is a niche sports scheduling story about the NHL adjusting to BC's move to permanent daylight time; it has some local relevance but offers little practical value or actionable information for daily life.
evaluation.persian_angle: این خبر عمدتاً دربارهٔ چالش‌های برنامه‌ریزی لیگ هاکی است و تأثیر عملی یا فوری روی زندگی روزمرهٔ ایرانیان ونکوور ندارد، هرچند تغییر ساعت تابستانی برای همه مرتبط است اما این مقاله اطلاعات کاربردی دربارهٔ آن ارائه نمی‌دهد.

item.title: 18-year-old who was shot dead in vehicle in Surrey identified
item.description: Homicide investigators have identified a man who was recently found shot dead in a vehicle that had hit a power pole in Surrey, B.C., as 18-year-old Gaurav Deol.
item.article_content:
Homicide investigators have identified a young man who was recently shot dead in a vehicle, which subsequently hit a power pole, in Surrey, B.C., as 18-year-old Gaurav Deol.

Deol was found in the area of 138 Street and 62 Avenue just before 12:30 a.m. PT on May 31. Police said he was pronounced dead at the scene.

On Tuesday, Sgt. Freda Fong with the Integrated Homicide Investigation Team (IHIT) said police were releasing Deol's identity as they work to understand his lifestyle and activities leading up to the fatal shooting.

No suspects have been named, but police believe the attack is targeted.

Man shot dead in vehicle in Surrey, police say

Fong said police are working closely with Deol's family.

"We have to remember that this is an 18-year-old very young man, and he leaves behind a grieving family. So despite the speculations, we would like to emphasize that the victim himself does not have a criminal record," she said.

"But we did uncover that he had recent contacts with members associated to organized crime, that is something that we will have to explore as a motivation," Fong added.

More stories from Vancouver-South Coast

The IHIT spokesperson confirmed that, shortly after Deol was found, a white Toyota SUV was found on fire nearby and it has been linked to the homicide.

Deol's death marked Surrey's 13th homicide of the year, according to the Surrey Police Service.

evaluation.reason_en: This is a straightforward crime identification story with no practical guidance, safety tips, or actionable information for the audience.
evaluation.persian_angle: این خبر صرفاً شناسایی قربانی یک پرونده جنایی است و هیچ اطلاعات کاربردی یا نکته ایمنی برای جامعه ایرانی ونکوور ارائه نمی دهد.

item.title: Man charged with 2nd-degree murder in fatal Langley, B.C., shooting
item.description: A man has been charged with second-degree murder in relation to a fatal shooting in Langley, B.C., last Tuesday.
item.article_content:
A man has been charged with second-degree murder in relation to a fatal shooting in Langley, B.C., last Tuesday.

Langley RCMP said officers were called to reports of shots fired on Brydon Crescent, near 55 Avenue and 200 Street, just after 11:30 p.m. PT on May 26.

There, police say they found a 47-year-old man who died despite life-saving efforts.

Homicide investigators said Tuesday that Justin Hopkins-Jones, 37, has been charged in relation to the death.

Investigators said they believe it was an isolated case.

Man dead in targeted Langley shooting, homicide investigators say

More stories from Vancouver-South Coast

"The matter is over a personal issue between the two. It's not believed to be related to gang activity or extortion attempts," said Sgt. Freda Fong with the Integrated Homicide Investigation Team (IHIT) in a Tuesday news conference.

Fong said police identified the accused within days of the shooting, thanks to witnesses and strong collaborative efforts among police agencies.

Police have not provided the identity of the victim. The shooting happened at an apartment building.

Hopkins-Jones's next court appearance is on June 5.

evaluation.reason_en: This is a localized crime report from a trusted source (CBC), but it offers little practical guidance or actionable information for the Persian-speaking community beyond general awareness of a past incident in Langley.
evaluation.persian_angle: این خبر مربوط به یک پرونده جنایی در لنگلی است و چون جزئیات کاربردی یا هشدار امنیتی مشخصی برای جامعه ایرانی‌زبان ونکوور ندارد، ارزش انتشار در این صفحه پایین است.
